docs.content:
# So Safe — Water Treatment System

Yohannes is building a web-based system for So Safe, a water treatment company. The client is a close personal connection.

The system they asked for has three roles: Technician (enters service dates only), Customer (sees their own cartridge schedule, gets SMS/email reminders), and Admin (full access, sends promotional ads, views overdue-replacement alarms). It tracks customer info, machine details, cartridge change dates, and service history. Next cartridge change is auto-calculated. Notifications go out to customers when a change is approaching and to the office when one is overdue.

This project is the initialization of that build.

# Discovery Phase: A Field Manual for the Aspiring AI Engineer & Tech Architect

## TL;DR
- **Discovery is the structured, time-boxed investigation at the start of a project that answers "are we solving the right problem, for the right people, in a way that's technically and economically feasible?"** It is the single highest-leverage skill you can build as a tech architect intern, because the Standish Group's CHAOS research consistently identifies unclear requirements and weak user involvement as the top causes of IT project failure, and a 2012 McKinsey/Oxford study of 5,400+ IT projects (Bloch, Blumberg & Laartz, *Delivering large-scale IT projects on time, on budget, and on value*) found that *"17% of IT projects perform so poorly that they potentially threaten the company's future"* — almost always traceable to discovery failures, not coding failures.
- **The discipline has converged across software, product, and consulting around a small set of frameworks you should learn by name**: Marty Cagan's four risks (value, usability, feasibility, business viability), the UK Government Digital Service (GDS) Discovery → Alpha → Beta → Live model, the British Design Council's Double Diamond, Google Ventures' 5-day Design Sprint, Teresa Torres' Continuous Discovery, Eric Ries' Lean Startup loop, and Rob Fitzpatrick's Mom Test. For AI/ML specifically, you add a data-readiness audit and a responsible-AI/ethics check on top.
- **As a solo or small-team developer in 2025-26, "minimum viable discovery" for a 2-week project is roughly half a day of stakeholder interviews + a one-page problem/scope/risk doc; for a multi-month build it scales up to a 1-4 week formal phase** with named deliverables (problem statement, user needs, technical spike, risk register, rough order-of-magnitude estimate, go/no-go recommendation). The pattern is the same — only the depth changes.

---

## Key Findings

1. **Discovery is not "requirements gathering" — it is risk reduction.** The mental shift Cagan introduced (around 2007, borrowing the term from pharmaceutical drug discovery) is that you go in *expecting* most ideas to be wrong, and your job is to find that out cheaply.
2. **The cost-of-change curve is the economic argument.** Industry rules of thumb (the IBM Systems Sciences Institute 1-10-100 rule) put the cost of fixing a defect at ~1x in planning, ~10x in development, and ~100x in production. Discovery is where bugs are cheapest to fix because they're still conversations, not code.
3. **Skipping discovery is the dominant failure mode in famous IT disasters.** HealthCare.gov (2013), Lidl/SAP (~€500M write-off), and Birmingham City Council's Oracle ERP (contributed to a 2023 Section 114 notice — effectively a local-government bankruptcy declaration) all share the same root cause: requirements and feasibility were never properly nailed down before build.
4. **AI/ML discovery is materially different.** Per RAND Corporation's August 2024 report *The Root Causes of Failure for Artificial Intelligence Projects and How They Can Succeed* (based on structured interviews with 65 experienced data scientists and engineers): *"more than 80% of AI projects fail to reach meaningful production deployment — exactly twice the failure rate of IT projects without AI components."* The added discovery work is a data audit, a model-feasibility spike, and an ethics/compliance check, on top of normal product discovery.
5. **There is a proven, publicly documented playbook you can copy: the GDS Service Manual.** The UK government has open-sourced an entire discovery methodology, including a real worked example (the GOV.UK Service Manual team's 4-week discovery in 2015) with named team members, interview counts, and verbatim findings.

---

## Details

### 1. The Big Picture: What Discovery Is and Why It Exists

**Definition.** Discovery is the first phase of the software development lifecycle (SDLC), preceding design and build, in which a team transforms a vague idea into a validated, scoped, estimated plan — or kills it. Its deliverables are knowledge, not code. The UK Government Digital Service puts it most plainly in the GOV.UK Service Manual: *"Before you commit to building a service, you need to understand the problem that needs to be solved… This part of your project is called the discovery phase."*

**Why it exists.** Three structural reasons:

1. **The cost of being wrong compounds.** A change made during discovery is a conversation; the same change during build is rework; in production it's an incident plus rework plus reputational damage. (IBM's 1-10-100 framing.)
2. **Most ideas don't work.** Marty Cagan, who coined the modern usage of the term at Silicon Valley Product Group, is blunt: *"At least half of your ideas are just not going to work… really good teams assume that at least three quarters of the ideas won't perform like they hope."* Discovery is how you find that out before spending the build budget.
3. **Stakeholders disagree without realizing it.** Discovery forces the implicit assumptions of business, users, and engineers into the same room.

**What happens when teams skip it.** The Standish Group's CHAOS research, spanning more than two decades and ~50,000 projects, consistently identifies "unclear/changing requirements" and "lack of user involvement" as the top two factors in failed and challenged projects. Their 2020 *Beyond Infinity* report found roughly 31% of US projects were cancelled and 53% challenged, leaving only ~16% fully successful. The McKinsey/Oxford 2012 study reinforces the point at the high end of risk: nearly one in five large IT projects performs badly enough to threaten the company itself.

**Where it sits in the lifecycle.** In a clean SDLC: **Discovery → Design/Architecture → Build → Test → Deploy → Operate**. In agile/product environments it never truly ends — Teresa Torres reframes it as **Continuous Discovery**, with at least weekly customer touchpoints running in parallel to delivery (dual-track agile). In waterfall/regulated environments it's a hard gate with a signed-off Software Requirements Specification (SRS).

**How discovery differs by context (the short version, expanded below):**

| Context | Typical length | Primary risk being reduced |
|---|---|---|
| Freelance/client work | 2 hours – 1 week | Scope and payment risk |
| Startup MVP | 1-4 weeks | Value risk (will anyone want this?) |
| Enterprise software | 4-12 weeks | Integration, change-management, ROI risk |
| AI/ML projects | 2-8 weeks | Data feasibility + model viability + ethics |
| Consulting engagement | 1-3 weeks (Big 4) | Diagnostic accuracy + scoping for SOW |

### 2. The Nine Components of Discovery (with Definitions)

**(a) Requirements gathering & stakeholder interviews.** Structured conversations with the people who own, fund, will use, will operate, or will be affected by the system. The goal is twofold: capture **functional requirements** (what the system does) and **non-functional requirements** (performance, security, scalability, accessibility, compliance). Best practice: separate the *problem* (what they need) from the *solution* (what they think they want).

**(b) Competitive analysis & market research.** Mapping competitors, substitutes, and adjacent products to understand the landscape. Includes feature benchmarking, pricing, positioning, and — crucially — *workarounds* (the spreadsheets, manual processes, or rival tools your users currently rely on). In government, this extends to mapping other departments doing similar work.

**(c) Technical feasibility assessment / technology evaluation.** Can it actually be built with available technology, skills, time, and money? This is usually a short **technical spike** — a proof-of-concept that de-risks the hardest unknown (a third-party API integration, a model accuracy target, a latency budget). For AI: can we hit the accuracy/latency/cost numbers the business case requires?

**(d) Risk assessment.** A register of what could go wrong, scored by likelihood and impact, with proposed mitigations. Categories: technical, schedule, cost, regulatory/legal, organizational, market, ethical. The McKinsey "red team" review of HealthCare.gov in March 2013 is a textbook example: they identified six top risks (evolving requirements, multiple definitions of success, contractor dependency, parallel-stacked phases, insufficient end-to-end testing, full-volume launch) — and were ignored.

**(e) Scope definition.** The bright line between *in scope* and *out of scope*, often expressed as a prioritized feature list (MoSCoW: Must / Should / Could / Won't) plus a Minimum Viable Product (MVP) definition. Scope is the single biggest predictor of whether estimates will hold.

**(f) User research / persona development.** First-hand contact with real users — interviews, observation, contextual inquiry, surveys, analytics. Outputs include user personas, journey maps, and a prioritized list of user needs. Rob Fitzpatrick's *The Mom Test* is the standard guide here: ask about the user's actual past behavior, not hypothetical opinions about your idea.

**(g) Architecture planning.** A high-level technical blueprint: major components, data flows, integration points, technology choices, deployment topology, and the key architectural decisions and their alternatives (often captured in lightweight Architecture Decision Records, ADRs). At discovery you're not designing in detail — you're proving the architecture is *plausible*.

**(h) Timeline and cost estimation.** Once scope and architecture are roughly known, the team produces a Rough Order of Magnitude (ROM) estimate, typically with an explicit confidence range (e.g., "±30%"). Vendor case studies commonly report that projects with thorough discovery achieve estimates within ~15% accuracy versus 200-300% overruns for projects that skip it — treat those numbers as directional, but the *direction* matches what Standish and McKinsey see across thousands of projects.

**(i) Discovery documentation / deliverables.** The tangible output. A canonical bundle (from Apriorit, Vention, and the GDS Service Manual):
- Validated problem statement / vision
- Prioritized user needs list
- Service map or as-is process map
- Prioritized scope / MVP definition
- High-level architecture diagram
- Risk register
- ROM cost & schedule estimate
- Go / no-go recommendation
- (Optional) clickable prototype or wireframes
- (For AI) data audit + model feasibility memo

### 3. A Proven Real-World Example: The GOV.UK Service Manual Discovery (UK Government Digital Service, 2015)

The UK Government Digital Service (GDS) has open-sourced its entire discovery methodology in the **GOV.UK Service Manual**, and the team that re-did the Service Manual itself in 2015 publicly documented their own discovery on three official `blog.gov.uk` posts. That makes it one of the cleanest external case studies you can study, because the methodology and the worked example are both public.

**The project.** In March 2015, GDS stood up a small multidisciplinary team of 8 — led by Nick Smith, with user researcher Patricia Bartholomeou and content lead Holly Garrett among them — to reassess who the users of the GOV.UK Service Design Manual were and what they needed. The original manual had launched in April 2013; two years on, the team wanted to test whether it was still meeting needs.

**Length: 4 weeks of discovery.** Bartholomeou wrote on the official GDS User Research blog: *"Our timeframe for discovery was short (just 4 weeks) and we wanted to speak to and observe as many users as possible."*

**Step-by-step how they ran it:**

1. **Framing and team set-up (Week 0).** The team was deliberately multidisciplinary — user researcher, information architect, content designers, product/delivery. Crucially, *the whole team* would do research, not just the researcher. They built a public Trello board so anyone in government could see what they were doing day-to-day.

2. **Desk research and data analysis (parallel, Weeks 1-3).** They analysed GOV.UK analytics, existing feedback forms, survey data, and the GitHub commit history of existing manual content before going into the field.

3. **User interviews across the country (Weeks 1-3).** Garrett wrote: *"The whole team went out and spoke to lots of people across government, visiting 7 locations over 3 weeks."* Bartholomeou put a precise number on it: *"Within 3 weeks, our team of 8 spoke to 29 people from London, Newcastle, Bristol, Birmingham, Coventry, Liverpool and Preston."* The non-researchers were upskilled with an internal "how-to" interviewing guide so they could contribute as note-takers and then lead sessions.

4. **Synthesis (Week 4).** *"Analysis involved us all coming together as a team with our 29 interview transcripts. This created a lot of fantastic insights (all captured on post-it notes), that we constantly moved and regrouped. We emerged with clear themes and some firm user needs."* A planned 1-day analysis ran into 2 days — an explicit lesson learned that they shared publicly.

5. **Findings — six themes.** Published 16 September 2015 on the Data in Government blog:
   - **Finding things is hard** — *"There used to be this thing… maybe it's gone?"*
   - **It is an introduction** — *"When I first started it was a great introduction. Its value becomes less over time."*
   - **Flexible, or not?** — *"The manual should explain where guidance is set in stone and where there is flexibility."*
   - **Evidence and examples** — *"I need to understand the rationale for GDS's interpretation."*
   - **Trust** — *"Keep the manual up to date or it rapidly becomes counter productive."*
   - **Persuasion or education** — a *new* user group emerged: people who used the manual to advocate for digital practices to colleagues *outside* service teams.

6. **Recommendation: proceed to Alpha.** The team moved into alpha with specific hypotheses to prototype against (notably: an information architect was brought on to address the findability theme). Garrett's published commitment: *"The strongest theme that emerged during the discovery research was users struggling to find what they needed in the manual. So we made finding things a key part of our focus for alpha."*

**Why this case is instructive for a tech architect intern:**
- It shows discovery is **time-boxed and disciplined**, not open-ended philosophizing.
- It shows the **whole team does research**, not just the user researcher — this is the modern norm (Cagan and Torres both call this the "Product Trio").
- It shows **deliverables are user needs and themes**, not Gantt charts or design mockups.
- It shows that "stopping" is a legitimate outcome — GDS explicitly tells teams *"It's not a failure to stop at the end of the discovery phase if your research shows that's the best thing to do. In fact, you'll be saving time and money that could be better spent elsewhere."*

(Other widely cited positive examples to be aware of: **Slack's pivot from Glitch (Tiny Speck, 2012-13)** — Stewart Butterfield's team used their failed game's internal communication tool, validated it iteratively by begging friends at Cozy and Rdio to try it, and identified a precise engagement threshold at which retention skyrocketed. As Butterfield told First Round Review in January 2015 (*"From 0 to $1B — Slack's Founder Shares Their Epic Launch Strategy"*), Slack's North Star metric was 2,000 messages sent by a team — and only after teams crossed that threshold did Slack publicly launch. **Airbnb's early discovery (2008-09)** — Brian Chesky famously took Paul Graham's advice "go to your users" literally, flying to New York to stay with hosts and learn what a "7-star experience" would look like. Both are pivot/discovery stories, not from-scratch discoveries, but they illustrate the same principles.)

### 4. Best Practices and Frameworks Worth Knowing by Name

**Frameworks to learn (and which to actually use when):**

- **Marty Cagan's Four Risks** (from *Inspired*, 2008): Value, Usability, Feasibility, Business Viability. Use as a checklist for every product idea.
- **Double Diamond** (UK Design Council, 2004/2005, after the Bánáthy divergence-convergence model): Discover → Define → Develop → Deliver, alternating divergent and convergent thinking across two diamonds. Use to structure the *shape* of a discovery.
- **Google Ventures Design Sprint** (Jake Knapp, *Sprint*, 2016): 5-day time-boxed discovery — Monday understand, Tuesday diverge, Wednesday decide, Thursday prototype, Friday test. Use when you need a sharp answer fast on a focused question.
- **GDS Discovery → Alpha → Beta → Live**: the gold standard for public-sector and regulated work, with public guidance at `gov.uk/service-manual`.
- **Lean Startup Build-Measure-Learn** (Eric Ries, 2011): MVPs as experiments to test the riskiest assumption. Use when value risk dominates.
- **Continuous Discovery Habits** (Teresa Torres, 2021): weekly customer touchpoints by the product trio, opportunity solution trees, assumption testing. Use to make discovery an *ongoing habit*, not a phase.
- **Jobs-to-be-Done** (Clayton Christensen / Tony Ulwick): frame user needs as the "job" they're hiring a product to do. Use to avoid building solutions in search of problems.
- **The Mom Test** (Rob Fitzpatrick, 2013, revised 2024): three rules for customer interviews — talk about their life not your idea, ask about specific past behaviour not hypotheticals, talk less and listen more.
- **Dual-Track Agile** (Jeff Patton, Marty Cagan): discovery and delivery as parallel, continuous tracks rather than sequential phases.

**What experienced architects, PMs, and designers actually do:**

1. **They time-box.** A discovery without an end date becomes "analysis paralysis." Pick 1-4 weeks (small/MVP), 4-8 weeks (mid), or 8-12 weeks (enterprise) up front.
2. **They send the whole team to talk to users.** Engineers and architects who personally interview users build dramatically better systems. The GDS team training their non-researchers to interview is the model.
3. **They write the problem before the solution.** A "press release" or "customer letter" (Cagan's variation on Amazon's "working backwards") forces clarity.
4. **They build a risk register on day one.** And they re-score it weekly.
5. **They run a technical spike on the riskiest unknown.** A two-day prototype against a real third-party API or a real dataset beats two weeks of architecture discussion.
6. **They produce one go/no-go recommendation, not a wall of documents.** The headline output is a decision, supported by evidence.
7. **They protect "stop" as a valid outcome.** A discovery that recommends *not* building something has saved the company money.
8. **They explicitly document assumptions.** "We assume the client will provide all images." Surfacing assumptions prevents "gotcha" moments in build.

### 5. Worst Practices / Anti-Patterns — Real Examples

**Anti-patterns:**

- **"Just-in-time spec writing."** Cagan's withering description: "a mad dash of just-in-time spec writing so that the engineers can be kept busy." Date is driving everything; nothing is being learned.
- **Asking leading questions.** "Would you use this?" almost always returns "yes" from polite people. Fitzpatrick's Mom Test fixes this.
- **One person doing all the customer conversations.** Creates a single point of interpretation and lets that person win all arguments with "it's what the customer wants."
- **Confusing discovery with documentation.** Producing 80-page Word documents nobody reads. The deliverable is *learning and decisions*, not page count.
- **Stacking phases in parallel under time pressure.** Building before requirements are stable, integrating before components are built, testing while still changing scope.
- **No "no-go" option.** If discovery is structurally incapable of recommending *stop*, it's theatre.
- **Treating discovery as a phase that ends forever.** Torres' point: 10 interviews then never talking to customers again is not discovery, it's an event.

**Famous failures traceable to discovery:**

- **HealthCare.gov (2013).** The US federal health insurance marketplace launched on 1 October 2013 and broke under load on day one. The US Government Accountability Office (GAO-14-694) found CMS *"issued task orders to develop the federally facilitated marketplace and federal data services hub systems when key technical requirements were unknown, including the number and composition of states to be supported and, importantly, the number of potential enrollees."* CGI Federal didn't receive substantial website specifications until **March 2013** — months before launch. Costs ballooned from a $93.7M initial estimate to over $500M by October 2013 and ultimately $1.7B (HHS Office of Inspector General). Classic discovery failure: requirements churned right up to launch, no MVP / phased rollout, no end-to-end testing time, no clear lead integrator.
- **Lidl × SAP (2011-2018).** The German grocer Lidl spent ~7 years and an estimated €500M attempting to replace its inventory system with SAP HANA-based eLWIS, and ultimately abandoned the project and reverted to its old system. A widely cited root cause in trade press was that Lidl insisted SAP adapt to Lidl's idiosyncratic practice of valuing inventory at purchase price rather than retail price — a fundamental requirements mismatch that a rigorous discovery phase should have surfaced and forced a decision on before contracts were signed.
- **Birmingham City Council Oracle ERP (UK, 2019-2023).** Originally budgeted around £20M, with costs reportedly escalating into the £100M+ range; the system went live in April 2022 with critical functionality non-functional, and the council issued a Section 114 notice (effectively declaring itself unable to balance its budget) in September 2023. Auditors and consultancies tied the failure to inadequate requirements understanding and weak governance.
- **IBM 7030 "Stretch" (1956-1961).** A historical reminder that "we underestimated what could be built in the time" is not a new problem — IBM missed performance targets so badly it had to cut the price by 60% and write off the program.

### 6. How Discovery Varies by Context

**Freelance / small client work.** The risk you are managing is mostly **scope and payment risk**. Discovery is typically a single discovery call plus a written **client discovery questionnaire** covering: company, audience, business goals, current solution, success metrics, budget range, hard deadlines, technical constraints, who has decision-making authority, what's explicitly *out* of scope. Output: a written Statement of Work the client signs before any code is written. The "devtimate" model is widely used: a paid mini-discovery (often $1-5K, or 5-10% of project value) before a full estimate. Skipping this is the #1 cause of freelance scope creep and unpaid rework.

**Startup MVP discovery.** The risk you are managing is **value risk** — does anyone want this? Lean Startup is the default frame: write down the riskiest assumption, build the smallest experiment to test it (landing page, concierge MVP, Wizard-of-Oz, demo video), measure, decide pivot-or-persevere. Iconic examples: Dropbox's 2008 demo video, which Drew Houston posted on Digg and Reddit — *"the beta waiting list jumped from 5,000 to 75,000 overnight"* — before Dropbox actually existed at scale; and Zappos founder Nick Swinmurn photographing shoes in local stores to test if anyone would buy shoes online before building inventory. Time budget: typically *days* per loop, weeks total. Read: *The Lean Startup*, *The Mom Test*, *Continuous Discovery Habits*.

**Enterprise / corporate software.** The risk you are managing is **integration, change-management, and ROI risk** — not "will users want it" (they're told to use it), but "will it integrate cleanly with the existing systems, will operations adopt it, and will the ROI hold up?" Discovery is typically 4-12 weeks, with formal SRS-style documentation, a steering committee, a clearly assigned system integrator, and a stage-gate before build. The HealthCare.gov, Lidl, and Birmingham failures all happened in this category. Cagan calls internal commercial products "hard" — the discipline of discovery applies even when the customer is captive.

**AI / ML projects specifically.** The RAND 2024 finding (>80% AI project failure rate, twice the non-AI IT rate) is the headline number that should orient your thinking. AI discovery adds three things on top of normal product discovery:

1. **Data discovery / data readiness audit.** Does the data actually exist? Is it accessible, legally usable, sufficiently large, and clean enough? Even the latest McKinsey *State of AI in 2025* survey — based on 1,993 respondents and finding that 88% of organizations report regular AI use in at least one business function — notes that *"nearly two-thirds have not yet begun scaling AI across the enterprise,"* with data readiness as a primary blocker. Assess on five dimensions: *context* (do you understand sources?), *clarity* (semantic correctness), *coverage* (volume), *credibility* (reliability), *capacity* (can you process at scale?).
2. **Model feasibility spike.** Build a quick baseline model on a small sample. If the baseline can't get within striking distance of the required accuracy/latency/cost, the project is not feasible — kill it now, not after six months of MLOps work. Towards Data Science's standard advice: break the ML project into granular steps each with an LOE range, build in quick wins early, and explicitly time-box the uncertain experiments.
3. **Ethics / responsible AI check.** Bias and fairness assessment of training data and outputs, privacy and security review (especially under GDPR, HIPAA, the EU AI Act), explainability requirements, human-oversight design, and ongoing monitoring plan. Frameworks: NIST AI Risk Management Framework, OECD AI Principles, Microsoft Responsible AI Standard. Recent academic work (e.g., the 2025 *DIP-AI: A Discovery Framework for AI Innovation Projects*, arXiv:2510.18017) is converging on a structured discovery phase specifically for AI, combining Design Thinking, CRISP-DM, and Lean.

For a tech architect intern moving into Applied AI, this third leg — ethics-aware discovery — is where you'll add disproportionate value early in your career, because most engineering teams still treat it as an afterthought.

**Consulting engagements.** Big 4 (Deloitte, EY, KPMG, PwC) and MBB (McKinsey, BCG, Bain) engagements almost always open with a 1-3 week diagnostic/discovery phase. Activities: stakeholder interviews up and down the org chart, document review, data analysis, workshops to co-create hypotheses, and a current-state assessment. Output: a problem statement, a hypothesis tree, a sized opportunity, and a scoped Statement of Work for the implementation. The discipline is so codified that "AI consulting discovery" now has standardized 5-phase methodologies across the major firms.

### 7. How to Actually Do Discovery as a Solo Developer or Small Team (2025-2026)

**The scaling principle.** The *components* of discovery don't change with project size — only the *depth* does. You always do problem, users, scope, feasibility, risk, estimate. For a 2-week project that's a one-page memo; for a 6-month project that's a 4-week phase.

**Minimum Viable Discovery (a 2-week build, solo or pair):** ~4-8 hours total.
- 30-60 min: 3-5 stakeholder/user conversations (apply The Mom Test: ask about past behaviour, not hypotheticals)
- 30 min: write a one-paragraph problem statement and define what success looks like in measurable terms
- 60 min: list the top 5 risks (technical, scope, dependency) and what would mitigate each
- 60-120 min: a technical spike on the riskiest unknown (e.g., does that API give you the data shape you need?)
- 30 min: define MVP scope using MoSCoW; explicitly list what's *out*
- 30 min: rough estimate with a confidence range
- Deliverable: a one-page README in the repo: Problem, Users, Scope, Out of Scope, Risks, Rough Estimate, Open Questions.

**Standard Discovery (a 2-3 month build, small team):** 1-2 weeks.
- 6-12 user/stakeholder interviews
- Competitive scan / existing-tools analysis
- Service map or user journey
- 2-3 day technical spike(s) on the hardest unknowns
- Risk register with owners
- Architecture sketch + ADRs for the 3-5 big technology decisions
- ROM estimate with ±30% range
- Go/no-go recommendation memo

**Larger Discovery (6+ month build):** 4-8 weeks, follow the GDS or dual-track agile playbook.

**Tools that earn their keep in 2026:**
- **Interviewing & synthesis:** Otter / Fireflies / Granola for AI-transcribed interviews; Dovetail or Notion for tagging and theming.
- **Customer-conversation discipline:** *The Mom Test* (Fitzpatrick); for AI-moderated user research at scale, tools like Koji or Outset.
- **Mapping & workshops:** Miro / FigJam / Mural for service maps, opportunity solution trees, and journey maps.
- **Prototyping:** Figma for UI; v0, Bolt, Lovable, or Cursor for clickable AI-built prototypes in hours.
- **Documentation:** Notion / Confluence / Linear; lightweight ADRs in your repo (`/docs/adr/`).
- **AI/ML discovery specifically:** Weights & Biases or MLflow for tracking feasibility experiments; data-profiling tools like ydata-profiling or great_expectations for the data audit.
- **For freelance:** Content Snare, HolaBrief, or a simple Notion form for client discovery questionnaires.

### 8. Essential Reading & Resources

**Books (in priority order for an aspiring Applied AI engineer):**
1. **Marty Cagan — *Inspired* (2017, 2nd ed.) and *Empowered* / *Transformed*.** The single best foundation in product thinking for engineers.
2. **Teresa Torres — *Continuous Discovery Habits* (2021).** The operating manual for ongoing discovery. Introduces the Product Trio and Opportunity Solution Trees.
3. **Rob Fitzpatrick — *The Mom Test* (2013, revised 2024).** Short, practical, transformative for customer interviews.
4. **Eric Ries — *The Lean Startup* (2011).** The MVP, Build-Measure-Learn, pivot-or-persevere vocabulary that everyone in tech uses.
5. **Jake Knapp, John Zeratsky, Braden Kowitz — *Sprint* (2016).** The 5-day Design Sprint playbook from Google Ventures.
6. **Donald Reinertsen — *The Principles of Product Development Flow*.** Heavier, more economics-driven; pairs well with your CS-and-Econ background.
7. **Clayton Christensen — *Competing Against Luck* (2016).** The canonical Jobs-to-be-Done text.

**Free / online resources:**
- **GOV.UK Service Manual** (`gov.uk/service-manual`) — free, world-class methodology you can copy.
- **Silicon Valley Product Group** (`svpg.com`) — Cagan's articles are all free.
- **Product Talk** (`producttalk.org`) — Teresa Torres' blog.
- **Google Ventures Design Sprint Kit** (`designsprintkit.withgoogle.com`).
- **The arXiv paper *DIP-AI: A Discovery Framework for AI Innovation Projects*** (2025) — current academic state of the art for AI-specific discovery.

---

## Recommendations (staged, for an intern starting now)

**This week:**
1. **Read** *The Mom Test* (3-4 hours, ~120 pages) and the GOV.UK Service Manual's discovery section (1 hour). These two alone will put you ahead of 90% of new engineers.
2. **Memorize Cagan's four risks** (Value, Usability, Feasibility, Business Viability). Every time someone proposes a feature in your internship, silently score it against the four. Eventually do it out loud.

**Your first month on the job:**
3. **In every project meeting, ask one discovery-style question:** "Who specifically is the user, and what evidence do we have they want this?" or "What's the riskiest assumption we're making?" You will quickly become known as the person who asks the right questions.
4. **Write a one-page discovery memo for your first assigned task** — Problem, Users, Scope, Out of Scope, Risks, Estimate — even if no one asked for it. Share it with your manager. It costs you an hour and signals seriously above-grade thinking.
5. **Volunteer to take notes at any user interview or stakeholder workshop.** This is the fastest way for an intern to be invited into real discovery work.

**Months 2-6:**
6. **Read *Inspired* and *Continuous Discovery Habits*.** Apply the Product Trio model (Eng + Design + PM) wherever you can in your team.
7. **Run one Design Sprint, even informally.** Pitch a 3-day version for a small problem your team is debating.
8. **Build an AI-specific discovery checklist for yourself** that includes the five data-readiness dimensions, a model-feasibility spike template, and a responsible-AI checklist (NIST AI RMF is a good starting frame).

**This year:**
9. **Document a discovery you led or contributed to** — write it up internally or externally (the GDS blog approach). This is the artifact that converts your internship into the next role.
10. **Track a thresholds-changing benchmark:** if any project you're on hits unclear requirements + changing scope + missed first internal demo, escalate it as a discovery gap. Those three together are the canonical CHAOS-report failure signal.

**When to change these recommendations.** If you find yourself working primarily on internal enterprise systems (where users are captive), shift weight from *value risk* to *adoption and integration risk*. If you find yourself in a consultancy, shift weight from *MVP shipping* to *diagnostic and SOW-scoping skill*. If you find yourself in AI research/applied AI, shift weight to *data readiness, model feasibility spikes, and ethics review*.

---

## Caveats

- **Vendor blogs over-claim ROI.** Many of the discovery-phase ROI numbers floating around the web ("60% fewer change requests," "15% estimate accuracy vs. 200-300% overruns") come from consultancy marketing pages — directionally consistent with academic findings but not independently audited. Treat them as orders of magnitude, not precise figures. The Standish CHAOS and McKinsey/Oxford figures are more solid.
- **Discovery can be over-done.** Cagan explicitly warned about teams that "believe strongly in Product Discovery but waste precious time by not planning their work." A 12-week discovery for a 4-week build is malpractice in the other direction.
- **"Continuous Discovery" ≠ "endless discovery."** Torres' insistence on weekly touchpoints can mutate into a discovery-only feature factory if delivery is not also held to schedule. Dual-track agile requires both tracks to ship.
- **The Slack and Airbnb stories are survivorship bias.** They're beautiful examples but also rare ones — for every Slack pivot there are dozens of Glitches that simply died. Use them for *technique* (Butterfield's 2,000-messages metric, Chesky's 7-star exercise), not as evidence that pivots usually work.
- **AI failure statistics are still maturing.** Be careful not to conflate *adoption* numbers (e.g., McKinsey's 88% of orgs using AI in some function) with *deployment success* numbers (e.g., RAND's >80% project failure rate to reach production). They measure different things; the gap between them is the discovery opportunity.
- **One person's discovery is another's procrastination.** The right amount of discovery is the smallest amount that materially reduces risk for the size and reversibility of the decision being made. Architect-level judgement is knowing where that line is — which is the skill this guide exists to help you build.
